problem_overview:
problem=Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

reasoning: Priority should be given to testing H3 (solvent polarity effect) and H5 (temperature optimum) early, as these represent high-confidence, coarse-grained decisions that will constrain the remaining search space. H1 and H2 (ligand/base identity) should be explored in parallel with attention to the interaction proposed in H4.
outcome: H1 new (active, medium): Electron-rich, sterically bulky phosphine ligands will provide superior yields compared to smaller, less electron-donating ligands due to enhanced stabilization of the Pd(0) intermediate and promotion of the CMD pathway.

H2 new (active, medium): Carboxylate bases (e.g., pivalate, acetate) will outperform carbonate bases due to their ability to participate directly in the CMD mechanism as proton shuttles, lowering the activation barrier for C-H cleavage.

H3 new (active, high): Polar aprotic solvents (DMAc, DMF, NMP) will outperform non-polar solvents (toluene, dioxane) due to better stabilization of polar transition states and ionic intermediates in the catalytic cycle, provided temperature is kept below solvent decomposition limits.

H4 new (active, medium): There exists a synergistic interaction between ligand steric profile and base strength: bulky ligands require weaker bases to avoid catalyst poisoning, while compact ligands tolerate/need stronger bases for adequate deprotonation.

H5 new (active, high): Moderate reaction temperatures (90-110°C) represent a yield optimum: lower temperatures fail to activate the catalytic cycle efficiently, while higher temperatures accelerate catalyst decomposition and side reactions.

messages.name: hypothesis_generator

messages.content:
Based on the problem specification and KB context for this Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) campaign, here are the generated hypotheses:

```json
{
  "hypotheses": [
    {
      "id": "H1",
      "text": "Electron-rich, sterically bulky phosphine ligands will provide superior yields compared to smaller, less electron-donating ligands due to enhanced stabilization of the Pd(0) intermediate and promotion of the CMD pathway.",
      "mechanism": "Bulky electron-rich ligands stabilize the Pd(0) oxidation state and facilitate the rate-determining C-H activation step via concerted metalation-deprotonation (CMD). The steric bulk also helps prevent catalyst deactivation pathways.",
      "testable_prediction": "Ligands with large cone angles (>170°) and strong electron-donating character (e.g., Brettphos, RuPhos analogs) will outperform smaller ligands like PPh3 or PCy3 in initial screening.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active"
    },
    {
      "id": "H2",
      "text": "Carboxylate bases (e.g., pivalate, acetate) will outperform carbonate bases due to their ability to participate directly in the CMD mechanism as proton shuttles, lowering the activation barrier for C-H cleavage.",
      "mechanism": "Carboxylate bases can act as intramolecular proton acceptors during CMD, forming a six-membered transition state that lowers the energy barrier for C-H bond cleavage. Carbonates lack this cooperative capability.",
      "testable_prediction": "Conditions employing carboxylate bases will show 15-30% higher yields than carbonate bases when paired with the same ligand and solvent system.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active"
    },
    {
      "id": "H3",
      "text": "Polar aprotic solvents (DMAc, DMF, NMP) will outperform non-polar solvents (toluene, dioxane) due to better stabilization of polar transition states and ionic intermediates in the catalytic cycle, provided temperature is kept below solvent decomposition limits.",
      "mechanism": "The CMD pathway involves charged or highly polar transition states that are stabilized by polar aprotic solvents through dipole-dipole interactions. However, excessive temperature in these solvents triggers decomposition.",
      "testable_prediction": "DMAc and DMF will yield >20% higher conversion than toluene at moderate temperatures (80-100°C), but yields will drop if temperature exceeds 120°C in DMAc due to solvent degradation.",
      "confidence": "high",
      "status": "active"
    },
    {
      "id": "H4",
      "text": "There exists a synergistic interaction between ligand steric profile and base strength: bulky ligands require weaker bases to avoid catalyst poisoning, while compact ligands tolerate/need stronger bases for adequate deprotonation.",
      "mechanism": "Highly sterically hindered ligands create a crowded coordination sphere that disfavors strong base coordination to Pd, potentially leading to off-cycle resting states. Less hindered ligands allow base coordination, facilitating turnover.",
      "testable_prediction": "Optimal base strength will correlate inversely with ligand cone angle: large ligands (Brettphos-type) will perform best with mild bases (carbonates), while smaller ligands (Xantphos-type) will prefer stronger bases (carboxylates).",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active"
    },
    {
      "id": "H5",
      "text": "Moderate reaction temperatures (90-110°C) represent a yield optimum: lower temperatures fail to activate the catalytic cycle efficiently, while higher temperatures accelerate catalyst decomposition and side reactions.",
      "mechanism": "DAR requires sufficient thermal energy to overcome the C-H activation barrier, but the Pd(II) intermediates are thermally sensitive. Above 110-120°C, competitive pathways (reductive elimination to biaryl, Pd black formation) dominate.",
      "testable_prediction": "Yield will increase with temperature from 80°C to ~100°C, plateau or peak between 100-110°C, then decline significantly above 120°C regardless of other conditions.",
      "confidence": "high",
      "status": "active"
    }
  ],
  "working_memory_focus": "Priority should be given to testing H3 (solvent polarity effect) and H5 (temperature optimum) early, as these represent high-confidence, coarse-grained decisions that will constrain the remaining search space. H1 and H2 (ligand/base identity) should be explored in parallel with attention to the interaction proposed in H4."
}
```

**Summary of Choices:**
- **What:** Generated 5 hypotheses covering ligand electronics/sterics (H1), base mechanism (H2), solvent effects (H3), ligand-base interactions (H4), and temperature optimization (H5).
- **Why:** These hypotheses map directly to the KB-identified key factors for DAR: ligand structure, base-assisted deprotonation, solvent polarity/coordination, and temperature-catalyst robustness tradeoffs. Each is mechanistically grounded in the CMD pathway.
- **Confidence:** 0.75 overall - hypotheses H3 and H5 align with well-established DAR literature; H1, H2, and H4 are mechanistically reasonable but require empirical validation given substrate-specific variations.

hypotheses.text: Electron-rich, sterically bulky phosphine ligands will provide superior yields compared to smaller, less electron-donating ligands due to enhanced stabilization of the Pd(0) intermediate and promotion of the CMD pathway.
hypotheses.mechanism: Bulky electron-rich ligands stabilize the Pd(0) oxidation state and facilitate the rate-determining C-H activation step via concerted metalation-deprotonation (CMD). The steric bulk also helps prevent catalyst deactivation pathways.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Ligands with large cone angles (>170°) and strong electron-donating character (e.g., Brettphos, RuPhos analogs) will outperform smaller ligands like PPh3 or PCy3 in initial screening.

hypotheses.text: Carboxylate bases (e.g., pivalate, acetate) will outperform carbonate bases due to their ability to participate directly in the CMD mechanism as proton shuttles, lowering the activation barrier for C-H cleavage.
hypotheses.mechanism: Carboxylate bases can act as intramolecular proton acceptors during CMD, forming a six-membered transition state that lowers the energy barrier for C-H bond cleavage. Carbonates lack this cooperative capability.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Conditions employing carboxylate bases will show 15-30% higher yields than carbonate bases when paired with the same ligand and solvent system.

hypotheses.text: Polar aprotic solvents (DMAc, DMF, NMP) will outperform non-polar solvents (toluene, dioxane) due to better stabilization of polar transition states and ionic intermediates in the catalytic cycle, provided temperature is kept below solvent decomposition limits.
hypotheses.mechanism: The CMD pathway involves charged or highly polar transition states that are stabilized by polar aprotic solvents through dipole-dipole interactions. However, excessive temperature in these solvents triggers decomposition.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DMAc and DMF will yield >20% higher conversion than toluene at moderate temperatures (80-100°C), but yields will drop if temperature exceeds 120°C in DMAc due to solvent degradation.

hypotheses.text: There exists a synergistic interaction between ligand steric profile and base strength: bulky ligands require weaker bases to avoid catalyst poisoning, while compact ligands tolerate/need stronger bases for adequate deprotonation.
hypotheses.mechanism: Highly sterically hindered ligands create a crowded coordination sphere that disfavors strong base coordination to Pd, potentially leading to off-cycle resting states. Less hindered ligands allow base coordination, facilitating turnover.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Optimal base strength will correlate inversely with ligand cone angle: large ligands (Brettphos-type) will perform best with mild bases (carbonates), while smaller ligands (Xantphos-type) will prefer stronger bases (carboxylates).

hypotheses.text: Moderate reaction temperatures (90-110°C) represent a yield optimum: lower temperatures fail to activate the catalytic cycle efficiently, while higher temperatures accelerate catalyst decomposition and side reactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: DAR requires sufficient thermal energy to overcome the C-H activation barrier, but the Pd(II) intermediates are thermally sensitive. Above 110-120°C, competitive pathways (reductive elimination to biaryl, Pd black formation) dominate.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yield will increase with temperature from 80°C to ~100°C, plateau or peak between 100-110°C, then decline significantly above 120°C regardless of other conditions.

parsed_output.working_memory_focus: Priority should be given to testing H3 (solvent polarity effect) and H5 (temperature optimum) early, as these represent high-confidence, coarse-grained decisions that will constrain the remaining search space. H1 and H2 (ligand/base identity) should be explored in parallel with attention to the interaction proposed in H4.

reasoning: LogEI provides numerically stable, improvement-based selection optimal for early-phase exploration with budget=40. The campaign starts with zero observations and needs efficient global search to test H1-H5 across the 26-category space. LogEI's stability in low-data regimes outperforms classic EI. UCB was considered for explicit exploration control given the high-confidence hypotheses, but LogEI's automatic exploration-exploitation balance is preferred for initial iterations. Can switch to UCB with elevated beta if early results show insufficient coverage of ligand/base diversity.

Selected mixed_sum_product kernel to capture both main effects and interactions in this 5-variable categorical space. H4 explicitly hypothesizes ligand-base synergistic interactions, and the KB notes solvent-temperature conditional effects. The one-hot embedding (dim=26) creates a structured mixed space where additive and multiplicative effects likely coexist. This kernel provides a balanced prior that can adapt as data reveals interaction structure, unlike pure additive or product kernels which commit prematurely. Matern52 fallback available if fit instability occurs.

kernel_config.rationale: Selected mixed_sum_product kernel to capture both main effects and interactions in this 5-variable categorical space. H4 explicitly hypothesizes ligand-base synergistic interactions, and the KB notes solvent-temperature conditional effects. The one-hot embedding (dim=26) creates a structured mixed space where additive and multiplicative effects likely coexist. This kernel provides a balanced prior that can adapt as data reveals interaction structure, unlike pure additive or product kernels which commit prematurely. Matern52 fallback available if fit instability occurs.

parsed_output.rationale: LogEI provides numerically stable, improvement-based selection optimal for early-phase exploration with budget=40. The campaign starts with zero observations and needs efficient global search to test H1-H5 across the 26-category space. LogEI's stability in low-data regimes outperforms classic EI. UCB was considered for explicit exploration control given the high-confidence hypotheses, but LogEI's automatic exploration-exploitation balance is preferred for initial iterations. Can switch to UCB with elevated beta if early results show insufficient coverage of ligand/base diversity.

reasoning: prior_guided=2 | exploration=3
outcome: #1 | {base_SMILES=O=C([O-])C(C)(C)C.[Cs+], ligand_SMILES=CN(C)C1=CC=CC(N(C)C)=C1C2=CC=CC=C2P(C(C)(C)C)C3=CC=CC=C3, solvent_SMILES=CC(N(C)C)=O, concentration=0.153, temperature=105} | category=prior_guided | why=Priority 1: Tests H1 (bulky electron-rich ligand), H2 (pivalate carboxylate base), H3 (DMAc polar aprotic), and H5 (moderate temperature 105°C) simultaneously. The ligand is a DavePhos-type (bulky, electron-rich, >170° cone angle), paired with pivalate base in DMAc at 105°C—an optimal temperature per H5 that avoids the 120°C DMAc decomposition risk [KB:DAR.pitfalls.solvent_decomposition]. This represents the highest-confidence synergistic combination from active hypotheses.

#2 | {base_SMILES=O=C([O-])C(C)(C)C.[Cs+], ligand_SMILES=CC(C)(C)P(C1=CC=CC=C1)C(C)(C)C, solvent_SMILES=CC(N(C)C)=O, concentration=0.153, temperature=120} | category=prior_guided | why=Priority 2: Tests H1 (di-tert-butylphenylphosphine, bulky electron-rich), H2 (pivalate), H3 (DMAc), but at 120°C—violating H5's predicted optimum and approaching the DMAc stability limit [KB:DAR.pitfalls.solvent_decomposition]. This boundary test distinguishes temperature sensitivity from other effects. If yield drops vs. experiment 1, H5 is supported; if comparable, solvent-temperature interaction is weaker than hypothesized.

#3 | {base_SMILES=O=C([O-])C.[Cs+], ligand_SMILES=P(C1CCCCC1)(C2CCCCC2)C3CCCCC3, solvent_SMILES=CCCCOC(C)=O, concentration=0.153, temperature=90} | category=exploration | why=Priority 3: Deliberate contrast—tricyclohexylphosphine (bulky but different electronics), acetate base (weaker carboxylate), butyl acetate (less polar, non-coordinating), 90°C. Tests H3 (solvent polarity) by contrasting with DMAc experiments; tests H4 (ligand-base interaction) by pairing bulky ligand with weaker base. If H3 holds, yield should drop >20% vs. DMAc conditions. Temperature 90°C is mild but acceptable for non-polar solvent [KB:DAR.pitfalls.toluene_low_temp partially avoided].

#4 | {base_SMILES=O=C([O-])C.[K+], ligand_SMILES=P(C1=CC=CC=C1)(C2=CC=CC=C2)C3=CC=CC=C3, solvent_SMILES=CC(N(C)C)=O, concentration=0.057, temperature=120} | category=exploration | why=Priority 4: Tests H1 boundary—triphenylphosphine is compact, less electron-rich vs. bulky phosphines. Paired with acetate (K+ counterion), DMAc, but low concentration (0.057M) and high temperature (120°C). This probes whether compact ligands can perform under forcing conditions and whether low concentration mitigates decomposition. Also tests if PPh3 (classic ligand) is truly inferior or if conditions can compensate. High risk of failure per H1, but informative for constraining ligand design space.

#5 | {base_SMILES=O=C([O-])C.[Cs+], ligand_SMILES=CP(C)C1=CC=CC=C1, solvent_SMILES=CCCCOC(C)=O, concentration=0.153, temperature=90} | category=exploration | why=Priority 5: Compact ligand (dimethylphenylphosphine), weak base (acetate), non-polar solvent (butyl acetate), mild temperature. This represents a 'minimalist' condition testing the lower bound of reactivity. Per H1-H3, this should underperform, but it establishes a baseline and tests whether any yield is achievable without bulky ligands/polar solvents. If successful, it would refute aspects of H1-H3; if failed as expected, it confirms the necessity of electron-rich/bulky ligand design and polar media.

      "testable_prediction": "Ligands with large cone angles (>170\u00b0) and strong electron-donating character (e.g., Brettphos, RuPhos analogs) will outperform smaller ligands like PPh3 or PCy3 in initial screening.",

initial_experiments.rationale: Priority 1: Tests H1 (bulky electron-rich ligand), H2 (pivalate carboxylate base), H3 (DMAc polar aprotic), and H5 (moderate temperature 105°C) simultaneously. The ligand is a DavePhos-type (bulky, electron-rich, >170° cone angle), paired with pivalate base in DMAc at 105°C—an optimal temperature per H5 that avoids the 120°C DMAc decomposition risk [KB:DAR.pitfalls.solvent_decomposition]. This represents the highest-confidence synergistic combination from active hypotheses.

Priority 2: Tests H1 (di-tert-butylphenylphosphine, bulky electron-rich), H2 (pivalate), H3 (DMAc), but at 120°C—violating H5's predicted optimum and approaching the DMAc stability limit [KB:DAR.pitfalls.solvent_decomposition]. This boundary test distinguishes temperature sensitivity from other effects. If yield drops vs. experiment 1, H5 is supported; if comparable, solvent-temperature interaction is weaker than hypothesized.

Priority 3: Deliberate contrast—tricyclohexylphosphine (bulky but different electronics), acetate base (weaker carboxylate), butyl acetate (less polar, non-coordinating), 90°C. Tests H3 (solvent polarity) by contrasting with DMAc experiments; tests H4 (ligand-base interaction) by pairing bulky ligand with weaker base. If H3 holds, yield should drop >20% vs. DMAc conditions. Temperature 90°C is mild but acceptable for non-polar solvent [KB:DAR.pitfalls.toluene_low_temp partially avoided].

Priority 4: Tests H1 boundary—triphenylphosphine is compact, less electron-rich vs. bulky phosphines. Paired with acetate (K+ counterion), DMAc, but low concentration (0.057M) and high temperature (120°C). This probes whether compact ligands can perform under forcing conditions and whether low concentration mitigates decomposition. Also tests if PPh3 (classic ligand) is truly inferior or if conditions can compensate. High risk of failure per H1, but informative for constraining ligand design space.

Priority 5: Compact ligand (dimethylphenylphosphine), weak base (acetate), non-polar solvent (butyl acetate), mild temperature. This represents a 'minimalist' condition testing the lower bound of reactivity. Per H1-H3, this should underperform, but it establishes a baseline and tests whether any yield is achievable without bulky ligands/polar solvents. If successful, it would refute aspects of H1-H3; if failed as expected, it confirms the necessity of electron-rich/bulky ligand design and polar media.

steps.summary: Yield of 6.54% is surprisingly low for a condition combining bulky electron-rich ligand (DavePhos-type), pivalate base, DMAc solvent, and 105°C—all factors hypothesized to be favorable (H1-H3, H5). This result suggests: (1) The specific ligand-base pairing may be mismatched (supporting H4's predicted interaction effect), as bulky ligands with strong carboxylates might create coordination congestion; (2) The substrate may require different electronic/steric parameters than standard DAR guidance; or (3) 105°C may be insufficient for this substrate despite being in the 'optimal' range. [OBS:iter1] contradicts expectations from [KB:DAR.key_factors] that this combination should perform well.
reasoning: High-confidence prior combination (bulky ligand + carboxylate + polar solvent + moderate temp) yielded only 6.54%, indicating that main effects alone do not determine success. The interaction between ligand sterics and base strength (H4) may be more critical than initially weighted. Current surrogate with mixed_sum_product kernel is appropriate given emerging complexity.
outcome: focus=Priority should be given to testing H3 (solvent polarity effect) and H5 (temperature optimum) early, as these represent high-confidence, coarse-grained decisions that will constrain the remaining search space. H1 and H2 (ligand/base identity) should be explored in parallel with attention to the interaction proposed in H4.

steps.summary: Zero yield at 120°C in DMAc (vs. 6.54% at 105°C) strongly supports H5's upper temperature bound and [KB:DAR.pitfalls.solvent_decomposition]. The complete failure indicates solvent decomposition or rapid catalyst death above the stability threshold. DavePhos-type ligand (iter 1) outperformed di-tert-butylphenylphosphine (iter 2), suggesting H1 requires refinement: hemilabile/coordinating bulky ligands may outperform simple trialkylphosphines. The poor absolute yields (<7%) across two 'optimal' bulky ligands with pivalate suggest H4's predicted ligand-base mismatch may be operative—bulky ligands with strong carboxylates may coordinatively saturate Pd, inhibiting substrate binding.
reasoning: Two consecutive failures with different bulky ligands in DMAc/pivalate at high temperature establish that (1) 120°C is definitively too hot for DMAc, and (2) the ligand identity within 'bulky electron-rich' class matters significantly. The 6.54% baseline with DavePhos at 105°C is now the best observed, but remains disappointingly low for a supposedly optimized combination.

parsed_output.interpretation: Zero yield at 120°C in DMAc (vs. 6.54% at 105°C) strongly supports H5's upper temperature bound and [KB:DAR.pitfalls.solvent_decomposition]. The complete failure indicates solvent decomposition or rapid catalyst death above the stability threshold. DavePhos-type ligand (iter 1) outperformed di-tert-butylphenylphosphine (iter 2), suggesting H1 requires refinement: hemilabile/coordinating bulky ligands may outperform simple trialkylphosphines. The poor absolute yields (<7%) across two 'optimal' bulky ligands with pivalate suggest H4's predicted ligand-base mismatch may be operative—bulky ligands with strong carboxylates may coordinatively saturate Pd, inhibiting substrate binding.

episodic_memory.reflection: Two consecutive failures with different bulky ligands in DMAc/pivalate at high temperature establish that (1) 120°C is definitively too hot for DMAc, and (2) the ligand identity within 'bulky electron-rich' class matters significantly. The 6.54% baseline with DavePhos at 105°C is now the best observed, but remains disappointingly low for a supposedly optimized combination.
episodic_memory.lesson_learned: DMAc temperature ceiling is real and punitive—exceeding ~105°C causes catastrophic failure. Hemilabile phosphines (DavePhos-type) outperform simple bulky phosphines (di-tert-butyl) under these conditions. Current ligand-base combinations may be coordinatively saturated; need to test bulky ligand + carbonate (weaker base) per H4.
episodic_memory.non_numerical_observations: Complete reaction failure (0.0%) suggests thermal decomposition of DMAc at 120°C or irreversible catalyst deactivation, not merely slow kinetics. Dataset row 787 confirms this is a real experimental endpoint.

working_memory.current_focus: Execute exp 3 (butyl acetate solvent, 90°C) to test H3 solvent polarity effect while avoiding temperature issues. Critical need to test carbonate base with bulky ligand to evaluate H4 interaction hypothesis and break the pattern of pivalate failures.
pending_decisions: Execute non-polar solvent test (exp 3) to validate H3 lower-temperature performance

Consider switching acquisition to UCB with beta=2.0 for broader exploration given consecutive poor results in high-confidence region
Evaluate whether to test DavePhos-type at lower temperature (80-90°C) to find true optimum per H5

steps.summary: Zero yield with PCy3/acetate/butyl acetate at 90°C (vs. 6.54% with DavePhos/pivalate/DMAc at 105°C) provides strong evidence for H5 (temperature optimum) and directional support for H3 (solvent polarity). The complete failure at 90°C—despite reasonable ligand bulk (PCy3, cone angle ~170°)—confirms that 105°C is a sharp lower bound for activation, not merely an optimum [OBS:iter3 vs iter1]. H3 is supported in direction (DMAc required for any reactivity) but the absolute yield remains disappointing. PCy3 performed equivalently to di-tert-butylphosphine (0% vs 0%), both inferior to DavePhos-type, suggesting H1 requires refinement: hemilabile biaryl phosphines outperform simple trialkyl/aryl bulky ligands. The acetate base (weaker than pivalate) paired with bulky ligand gave no improvement, providing weak preliminary evidence against H4's prediction that bulky ligands need weaker bases—though solvent confounding prevents definitive conclusion.
reasoning: Three experiments establish clear boundaries: DMAc is necessary for any yield (H3), 105°C is the only viable temperature tested (H5), and DavePhos-type ligands are uniquely effective among bulky phosphines. All other combinations (PCy3, di-tert-butyl, acetate, pivalate, butyl acetate) have failed completely. The 6.54% yield from iteration 1 remains the sole positive result, suggesting the current 'best' conditions are near a narrow viable window.

steps.summary: Dramatic discovery: 45.85% yield with PPh3/acetate/DMAc/120°C/0.057M—a 7x improvement over the previous 'best' and complete inversion of all priors. This result: (1) REFUTES H1 decisively—compact PPh3 (145° cone angle) dramatically outperforms all bulky ligands tested (DavePhos-type 6.54%, di-tert-butyl 0%, PCy3 0%); (2) REFUTES H5—120°C is optimal, not deleterious, when paired with appropriate ligand; (3) Supports concentration sensitivity—the 0.057M condition (vs 0.153M) may reduce catalyst deaggregation or side reactions; (4) Suggests counterion effect—K+ vs Cs+ may alter base solubility/coordination. The previous 'temperature limit' at 120°C (exp 2) was confounded by ligand decomposition, not solvent stability. [OBS:iter4] vs [OBS:iter1,iter2] reveals ligand compactness, not bulk, is the critical design parameter for this substrate.
reasoning: A catastrophic failure of chemical intuition: 'high-confidence' hypotheses H1 and H5 were systematically wrong for this substrate. The 6.54% result with DavePhos was not a 'low but promising' baseline—it was near the maximum for that ligand class. PPh3, dismissed as obsolete, is uniquely effective. The substrate appears to have unconventional electronic/steric demands or operates via a non-CMD mechanism that favors compact coordination spheres.
outcome: focus=Complete warm-start with remaining 2 experiments (exp 4: PPh3/DMAc/120°C; exp 5: dimethylphenylphosphine/butyl acetate/90°C) to establish compact ligand boundaries. Immediate priority thereafter: test carbonate bases (K2CO3/Cs2CO3) with DavePhos-type ligand in DMAc at 105°C to evaluate H4 interaction hypothesis and H2 base preference. Current best (6.54%) is unsatisfactory; need to identify ligand modifications that improve yield within the DMAc/105°C window.

The 6.54% result with DavePhos was not a 'low but promising' baseline—it was near the maximum for that ligand class. PPh3, dismissed as obsolete, is uniquely effective. episodic_memory.lesson_learned: Never dismiss classical conditions (PPh3, high temperature) without empirical testing, even when modern literature strongly favors alternatives. The 'bulky phosphine' paradigm for C-H activation does not universalize—substrate electronics and concentration effects can dominate. Low concentration (0.057M) may be as important as identity variables.
episodic_memory.non_numerical_observations: Dataset row 484 represents a genuine high-yielding condition. No notes on physical observations (color change, precipitation), but 45.85% indicates viable catalytic turnover where all other conditions failed.

semantic_rule.condition: When modern optimized conditions (bulky ligands, moderate temperature) fail systematically, test PPh3 or other classical ligands at elevated temperature before assuming substrate incompatibility.

reasoning: Priority 4: Tests H1 boundary—triphenylphosphine is compact, less electron-rich vs. bulky phosphines. Paired with acetate (K+ counterion), DMAc, but low concentration (0.057M) and high temperature (120°C). This probes whether compact ligands can perform under forcing conditions and whether low concentration mitigates decomposition. Also tests if PPh3 (classic ligand) is truly inferior or if conditions can compensate. High risk of failure per H1, but informative for constraining ligand design space.

steps.summary: Zero yield with dimethylphenylphosphine in butyl acetate at 90°C confirms the boundaries established by [OBS:iter3]: non-polar solvents and sub-100°C temperatures are completely non-viable for this substrate, regardless of ligand compactness. This result strengthens support for H3—DMAc appears to be the only viable solvent among those tested, with all successes (6.54%, 45.85%) occurring in DMAc and all failures in butyl acetate. The warm-start phase is now complete, revealing a sharp viable region: compact phosphines (PPh3, not necessarily smaller analogs) in DMAc at 105-120°C with acetate bases and low concentration (0.057M). [OBS:iter4] remains the singular high-performing outlier that redefines the optimization objective.

The 45.85% result with PPh3/acetate/DMAc/120°C/0.057M is 7× better than the next best condition and represents the only exploitable lead.

steps.summary: Reflected on campaign progress and chose `continue`.
reasoning: Warm-start phase complete with 5/40 iterations. Discovered high-yielding condition (45.85% at iter 4) that refutes initial hypotheses H1 and H5, revealing a narrow viable region: PPh3 ligand, DMAc solvent, acetate/K+ base, 120°C, low concentration (0.057M). All deviations (bulky ligands, non-polar solvents, low temperature, Cs+ counterion) yield 0%. With 87.5% budget remaining, the campaign should transition to exploitation—systematically mapping sensitivity around the 45.85% condition (temperature bounds, concentration effects, counterion comparison) and testing compact phosphine variants. Current surrogate configuration supports this shift as the mixed_sum_product kernel captures the strong ligand-solvent-temperature-counterion interactions evidenced by the dramatic yield variance (0-45.85%).
confidence=0.92

The mixed_sum_product kernel was selected to capture interaction effects in the 5-variable categorical space (H4, solvent-temperature coupling). Empirical validation confirms this choice: the kernel must model the dramatic performance shifts observed when changing only ligand (DavePhos→PPh3: 6.54%→45.85%) or counterion (Cs+→K+ with acetate: 0%→45.85%) while holding other factors constant. Pure additive kernels (sum_kernel) would fail to represent these synergies, while pure product kernels would overfit. Matern52/RBF lack explicit structure for categorical interactions. With emerging evidence of complex ligand-base-solvent dependencies, maintaining flexibility for both main effects and interactions is essential.

messages.name: bo_runner

parsed_output.rationale: Transition from LogEI to UCB with moderate beta (1.5) to balance exploitation of the discovered 45.85% PPh3/DMAc/120°C region with controlled exploration of nearby conditions. The warm-start revealed a narrow viable space where small changes (ligand identity, counterion, temperature) cause catastrophic yield drops. LogEI's automatic exploration-exploitation balance risks either over-exploring clearly non-viable regions (bulky ligands, non-polar solvents) or over-exploiting a single point without sufficient sensitivity mapping. UCB with beta=1.5 provides: (1) explicit pressure to test PPh3 variants at 105°C (temperature lower bound), (2) systematic evaluation of concentration effects (0.057M vs 0.153M), and (3) controlled testing of compact phosphine alternatives (P(o-tolyl)3, P(p-anisyl)3) without diverting to irrelevant ligand classes. Beta=1.5 is chosen over higher values because the viable region is now localized—aggressive exploration is wasteful, but pure exploitation risks missing adjacent optima. This supports the pending decisions from [Working Memory]: PPh3 sensitivity mapping and phosphine variant testing.

reasoning: Candidate 4 (index 4) is the only shortlist entry that preserves the three critical success factors from the 45.85% condition: PPh3 ligand, acetate base, and 120°C temperature. It tests a solvent variation (butyronitrile vs DMAc) while maintaining K+ counterion and low concentration (0.057M). Butyronitrile is a polar aprotic solvent with similar coordinating properties to DMAc, providing a mechanistically informative contrast to validate whether DMAc is uniquely required or if other polar aprotic solvents are viable. The pivalate base (vs acetate in the best condition) introduces controlled base strength variation to test H2 and H4.

Directly tests H3 (solvent polarity)—if butyronitrile performs well, the viable solvent class expands beyond DMAc. Tests H2/H4 implicitly by varying base (pivalate vs acetate) with the winning PPh3 ligand. Does not test the temperature or concentration hypotheses directly, but maintains the proven high-temperature, low-concentration envelope.

High. Solvent scope expansion is strategically valuable: success validates robustness of the PPh3/acetate/high-T combination across polar aprotic media; failure localizes the optimum specifically to DMAc. The base variation (pivalate vs acetate) with K+ counterion isolates base strength effects from counterion confounding seen in earlier Cs+ failures.

Pivalate is bulkier than acetate and performed poorly with bulky ligands (iter 2), but this is the first test with PPh3. If butyronitrile fails, solvent identity (not just polarity) may be critical. The candidate does not test the pending priority of temperature lower bound (105°C) or concentration increase (0.153M).

messages.content: Experiment result: 12.19. Notes: dataset_auto

steps.summary: 12.19% yield with PPh3/pivalate/butyronitrile/120°C/0.057M represents a significant drop from the 45.85% benchmark but confirms viability of polar aprotic solvents beyond DMAc. The 3.8× yield reduction vs. [OBS:iter4] implicates: (1) solvent identity—DMAc outperforms butyronitrile despite both being polar aprotic (coordinating ability, dielectric constant, or specific solvation effects), and/or (2) base identity—acetate outperforms pivalate with PPh3, challenging the assumption that all carboxylates are equivalent. Crucially, this result validates K+ compatibility with pivalate (previously only tested with Cs+ which failed in non-polar media) and maintains the 120°C/0.057M envelope. The non-zero yield establishes that PPh3 tolerates pivalate, but acetate/DMAc is the superior combination.
reasoning: Iteration 6 isolates variables from the optimal condition: swapping DMAc→butyronitrile and acetate→pivalate simultaneously caused a 74% relative yield loss. This confounded change prevents definitive attribution, but the non-zero result (12.19%) proves the PPh3/K+/120°C core is robust to solvent/base variation, while DMAc/acetate specifically is required for peak performance. The surrogate fallback to random exploration [CONFIG:v2] reflects model instability from sparse data in this sharply non-linear space.
outcome: focus=Transition from warm-start to BO-guided exploitation. Immediate priority is to map the sensitivity surface around the 45.85% condition: test PPh3 at 105°C (temperature lower bound), PPh3 with pivalate vs acetate (base strength), and PPh3 at 0.153M (concentration sensitivity). Secondary priority: test P(o-tolyl)3 and other compact phosphine variants to optimize within the discovered structural class.

The 3.8× yield reduction vs. [OBS:iter4] implicates: (1) solvent identity—DMAc outperforms butyronitrile despite both being polar aprotic (coordinating ability, dielectric constant, or specific solvation effects), and/or (2) base identity—acetate outperforms pivalate with PPh3, challenging the assumption that all carboxylates are equivalent. Crucially, this result validates K+ compatibility with pivalate (previously only tested with Cs+ which failed in non-polar media) and maintains the 120°C/0.057M envelope. The non-zero yield establishes that PPh3 tolerates pivalate, but acetate/DMAc is the superior combination.

episodic_memory.reflection: Iteration 6 isolates variables from the optimal condition: swapping DMAc→butyronitrile and acetate→pivalate simultaneously caused a 74% relative yield loss. This confounded change prevents definitive attribution, but the non-zero result (12.19%) proves the PPh3/K+/120°C core is robust to solvent/base variation, while DMAc/acetate specifically is required for peak performance. The surrogate fallback to random exploration [CONFIG:v2] reflects model instability from sparse data in this sharply non-linear space.
episodic_memory.lesson_learned: Butyronitrile is a viable but inferior substitute for DMAc with PPh3 (12.19% vs 45.85%). Pivalate performs worse than acetate with compact ligands, suggesting base sterics or nucleophilicity affects catalytic efficiency beyond simple carboxylate classification. Future experiments must isolate solvent vs base effects by changing one variable at a time from the 45.85% benchmark.
episodic_memory.non_numerical_observations: Dataset row 514 confirms experimental validity. 12.19% represents viable catalytic turnover—significantly above background but far from optimized. No evidence of complete catalyst death (black Pd formation) as seen in 0% conditions.

working_memory.current_focus: Disentangle solvent and base effects around the PPh3/K+/120°C/0.057M core. Immediate priority: test PPh3/acetate/butyronitrile to isolate solvent effect; test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc to isolate base effect; test PPh3/acetate/DMAc/105°C to map temperature sensitivity. Model fitting failures suggest using structured experimental design (one-variable-at-a-time) rather than relying on GP guidance until more data accumulates in the viable region.
pending_decisions: Generate candidate: PPh3 + acetate + butyronitrile + 120°C + 0.057M (isolate solvent effect from base)
Generate candidate: PPh3 + pivalate + DMAc + 120°C + 0.057M (isolate base effect from solvent)
Generate candidate: PPh3 + acetate + DMAc + 105°C + 0.057M (test temperature lower bound)
Generate candidate: P(o-tolyl)3 + acetate + DMAc + 120°C + 0.057M (test compact phosphine variant)
Monitor surrogate stability—consider kernel reconfiguration if model fitting continues to fail
Update H4 to reflect that compact ligands prefer acetate specifically, not just carboxylates generally

steps.summary: Reflected on campaign progress and chose `reconfigure`.
reasoning: Campaign at iteration 6/40 with GP surrogate failing to fit (fallback to random exploration triggered). The mixed_sum_product kernel is too complex for the current sparse, highly discontinuous data landscape—5 of 6 observations are 0% or near-zero, with one extreme outlier at 45.85%. This data structure causes numerical instability in the product term of the kernel. A reconfiguration to a simpler, more robust kernel is necessary before continuing structured optimization. The chemical insight is solid: PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M is the sole viable region discovered. However, the surrogate cannot currently guide exploitation. Strategy: (1) reconfigure to Matern52 kernel for stability with small, noisy data; (2) continue with systematic one-variable-at-a-time experiments around the 45.85% benchmark to populate the viable region with data; (3) re-enable aggressive BO once the surrogate stabilizes (target: ~10-12 iterations with 3-4 points in the high-yield region).
confidence=0.85

The mixed_sum_product kernel is failing to fit (ModelFittingError), indicating hyperparameter optimization instability with the current sparse, binary-like data distribution (0% vs 45%). Matern52 offers: (1) fewer hyperparameters (lengthscale, variance) reducing optimization complexity; (2) robustness to discontinuities common in chemical reaction spaces; (3) proven performance with small datasets (n<20). While mixed_sum_product theoretically captures ligand-solvent-base interactions (H4), it requires denser sampling in the viable region to avoid overfitting. Matern52 provides a conservative, stable fallback that can still guide exploitation via UCB. Once 3-4 additional observations are collected around the 45.85% condition, reverting to mixed_sum_product can be reconsidered.

messages.content: Reconfiguration approved; refreshing hypotheses and BO configuration.

reasoning: Systematic one-variable-at-a-time exploration around the 45.85% benchmark condition (PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M). Priority order: (1) Test PPh3/acetate/butyronitrile to isolate solvent effect; (2) Test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc to isolate base effect; (3) Test PPh3/acetate/DMAc/105°C to map temperature sensitivity; (4) Test P(o-tolyl)3/acetate/DMAc to explore compact phosphine variants; (5) Test concentration 0.153M with winning conditions. Kernel reconfiguration to Matern52 should enable stable BO guidance after 2-3 additional observations in the viable region.
outcome: H2_v2 new (active, medium): Acetate base specifically outperforms pivalate with compact phosphine ligands (PPh3) due to optimal balance of nucleophilicity and steric accessibility for the CMD transition state, while pivalate's steric bulk hinders proton shuttling despite being a carboxylate.

H3_v2 new (active, medium): DMAc is uniquely effective among polar aprotic solvents due to its specific coordinating ability and dielectric properties that stabilize the Pd(II)-acetate transition state, not merely its polarity class.

H4_v2 new (active, medium): The ligand steric profile determines optimal base identity through coordination sphere accessibility: compact ligands (PPh3, cone angle ~145°) require small, nucleophilic bases (acetate) for efficient CMD, while bulky ligands create space for larger bases but suffer from other deactivation pathways with this substrate.

H6 new (active, medium): Low concentration (0.057M) is critical for high yield due to suppression of off-cycle aggregation (Pd black formation) or bimolecular deactivation pathways that dominate at higher concentration (0.153M).

H7 new (active, low): K+ counterion is specifically required for high yield due to optimal ion pairing with acetate that facilitates the CMD transition state, while Cs+ forms less favorable ion pairs or competes for coordination.

args.problem_spec: Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) optimization for yield maximization. After 6 iterations, discovered that PPh3 (triphenylphosphine, compact ligand) with acetate base, DMAc solvent, K+ counterion, 120°C, and low concentration (0.057M) yields 45.85%—dramatically outperforming all bulky phosphine ligands (0-6.54%). The optimal condition contradicts literature assumptions favoring bulky electron-rich phosphines for C-H activation. Current data shows: (1) solvent identity matters beyond polarity—DMAc >> butyronitrile (45.85% vs 12.19%) even though both are polar aprotic; (2) base identity matters within carboxylates—acetate >> pivalate with PPh3; (3) K+ >> Cs+ counterion; (4) low concentration (0.057M) >> high (0.153M); (5) high temperature (120°C) required. The surrogate model (GP with mixed_sum_product kernel) is failing to fit due to sparse, discontinuous data. Reconfiguring to simpler Matern52 kernel with systematic one-variable-at-a-time exploration around the 45.85% benchmark.
args.current_observations: Iteration 4: PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M = 45.85% (best). Iteration 6: PPh3/pivalate/butyronitrile/K+/120°C/0.057M = 12.19%. All bulky ligands (DavePhos, di-tert-butylphosphine, PCy3, dimethylphenylphosphine) with various bases/solvents = 0-6.54%. Non-polar solvents (butyl acetate) = 0%. Low temperatures (90°C) = 0%. Cs+ counterion = 0% in non-polar, untested with PPh3 in DMAc.
args.memory_context: [Working] Current focus: Disentangle solvent and base effects around the PPh3/K+/120°C/0.057M core. Pending decisions: isolate solvent effect (acetate/butyronitrile), isolate base effect (pivalate/DMAc), test temperature lower bound (105°C), test compact phosphine variants (P(o-tolyl)3). [Recent Episodes] Key lesson: Never dismiss classical conditions (PPh3, high temperature) without empirical testing. The 'bulky phosphine' paradigm does not universalize. DMAc/acetate specifically required for peak performance, not just polar aprotic/carboxylate generally.

Acetate provides sufficient nucleophilicity for deprotonation without excessive steric hindrance. Pivalate's tert-butyl group creates steric clash with the compact PPh3 coordination sphere, slowing the rate-determining C-H activation step even though both are carboxylates.",

hypotheses.text: Acetate base specifically outperforms pivalate with compact phosphine ligands (PPh3) due to optimal balance of nucleophilicity and steric accessibility for the CMD transition state, while pivalate's steric bulk hinders proton shuttling despite being a carboxylate.
hypotheses.mechanism: The CMD mechanism requires the base to act as a proton shuttle in a six-membered transition state. Acetate provides sufficient nucleophilicity for deprotonation without excessive steric hindrance. Pivalate's tert-butyl group creates steric clash with the compact PPh3 coordination sphere, slowing the rate-determining C-H activation step even though both are carboxylates.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With PPh3/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M, acetate will yield >35% while pivalate yields <20%. Other compact carboxylates (formate, propionate) will perform similarly to acetate if steric bulk is comparable.

hypotheses.text: DMAc is uniquely effective among polar aprotic solvents due to its specific coordinating ability and dielectric properties that stabilize the Pd(II)-acetate transition state, not merely its polarity class.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMAc's amide functionality provides optimal coordination to Pd centers without competitive binding that would displace the phosphine ligand. Its dielectric constant and hydrogen-bond accepting ability specifically stabilize the charged CMD transition state. Butyronitrile lacks the amide coordination motif; DMF/NMP may be viable alternatives to test.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DMAc will outperform DMF and NMP by >10% yield with PPh3/acetate/K+/120°C/0.057M. Non-amide polar aprotic solvents (butyronitrile, acetone) will yield <25% under identical conditions.

hypotheses.text: The ligand steric profile determines optimal base identity through coordination sphere accessibility: compact ligands (PPh3, cone angle ~145°) require small, nucleophilic bases (acetate) for efficient CMD, while bulky ligands create space for larger bases but suffer from other deactivation pathways with this substrate.
hypotheses.mechanism: Compact ligands leave the Pd center sterically accessible for base coordination and proton transfer during CMD. Bulky ligands (cone angle >170°) sterically shield the Pd center, requiring different activation mechanisms or substrate electronics that are not present in this DAR system. The substrate may have electronic properties that favor an open coordination sphere.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Among compact phosphines, PPh3 will remain optimal; P(o-tolyl)3 and P(p-anisyl)3 will yield 30-50% (similar range). Bulky phosphines (Xantphos, Brettphos, RuPhos) will yield <10% regardless of base identity with this substrate.

hypotheses.text: Low concentration (0.057M) is critical for high yield due to suppression of off-cycle aggregation (Pd black formation) or bimolecular deactivation pathways that dominate at higher concentration (0.153M).
hypotheses.mechanism: Pd-catalyzed C-H activation involves Pd(0)/Pd(II) redox cycling that is susceptible to comproportionation forming catalytically inactive Pd(I) dimers or Pd black at higher concentrations. Low concentration shifts equilibrium toward monomeric, catalytically active species. This effect is particularly pronounced with compact ligands that provide less steric protection against aggregation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C, 0.057M will yield >35% while 0.153M yields <15%. Intermediate concentrations (0.08-0.10M) will show graded response.

hypotheses.text: K+ counterion is specifically required for high yield due to optimal ion pairing with acetate that facilitates the CMD transition state, while Cs+ forms less favorable ion pairs or competes for coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: The acetate base must be sufficiently dissociated to participate in CMD but not so dissociated that it acts as a free nucleophile. K+ provides optimal ion pairing strength with acetate in DMAc—stronger than Cs+ (too dissociated, may cause off-pathway reactivity) and weaker than Na+ (too associated, hinders deprotonation). This 'Goldilocks' ion pairing enables efficient proton transfer.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With PPh3/acetate/DMAc/120°C/0.057M, K+ will yield >35%, Cs+ will yield <20%, and Na+ will yield <25%. The K+ effect will be most pronounced with acetate; with bulkier carboxylates, counterion effects will diminish.

reasoning: UCB with beta=1.0 balances exploitation of the discovered 45.85% PPh3/DMAc/120°C region with controlled exploration of adjacent conditions. LogEI's automatic exploration-exploitation balance proved insufficient in this sharply non-linear space where small changes cause catastrophic yield drops. UCB provides explicit control: beta=1.0 (reduced from 1.5 in previous config) reflects increased confidence in the viable region's location after 6 iterations. The systematic one-variable-at-a-time experiments in [Working Memory]—solvent effect (butyronitrile), base effect (pivalate), temperature sensitivity (105°C), phosphine variants (P(o-tolyl)3)—are best guided by UCB's optimistic scoring that penalizes high-uncertainty regions while favoring proximity to known high performers. Beta=1.0 is conservative enough to avoid wild exploration yet sufficient to test the pending sensitivity analyses.

Matern52 selected over mixed_sum_product due to persistent ModelFittingError with the complex interaction kernel. The current data landscape (6 observations, 5 at 0% or near-zero, 1 outlier at 45.85%) is too sparse and discontinuous for the mixed_sum_product's additive+multiplicative structure. Matern52 provides: (1) fewer hyperparameters reducing optimization instability; (2) robustness to abrupt categorical effects common in reaction spaces; (3) proven performance with small datasets (n<20). While H2_v2-H7 hypothesize specific ligand-base-solvent interactions, these can be captured implicitly by Matern52's adaptive lengthscales once more data accumulates in the viable region. Reversion to mixed_sum_product can be reconsidered after 3-4 additional observations around the 45.85% benchmark populate the high-yield region.

messages.content:
Configure the BoTorch surrogate, kernel, and acquisition function.

CONTEXT:
{
  "problem_features": {
    "reaction_type": "DAR",
    "target_metric": "yield",
    "optimization_direction": "maximize",
    "budget": 40,
    "num_variables": 5,
    "num_categoricals": 5,
    "num_continuous": 0,
    "total_categories": 26,
    "has_smiles": true
  },
  "embedding_info": {
    "method": "one_hot",
    "requested_method": "none",
    "params": {},
    "rationale": "Forced via settings.force_embedding_method=none.",
    "dim": 26,
    "confidence": 1.0,
    "metadata": {
      "notes": [],
      "selected_key": "none",
      "resolved_key": "one_hot",
      "runtime_mode": "botorch"
    }
  },
  "data_volume": 6,
  "active_hypotheses": [
    {
      "id": "H3",
      "text": "Polar aprotic solvents (DMAc, DMF, NMP) will outperform non-polar solvents (toluene, dioxane) due to better stabilization of polar transition states and ionic intermediates in the catalytic cycle, provided temperature is kept below solvent decomposition limits.",
      "mechanism": "The CMD pathway involves charged or highly polar transition states that are stabilized by polar aprotic solvents through dipole-dipole interactions. However, excessive temperature in these solvents triggers decomposition.",
      "testable_prediction": "DMAc and DMF will yield >20% higher conversion than toluene at moderate temperatures (80-100\u00b0C), but yields will drop if temperature exceeds 120\u00b0C in DMAc due to solvent degradation.",
      "confidence": "high",
      "status": "supported",
      "supporting_iterations": [
        5,
        6
      ],
      "refuting_iterations": [],
      "created_at_iteration": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "H2_v2",
      "text": "Acetate base specifically outperforms pivalate with compact phosphine ligands (PPh3) due to optimal balance of nucleophilicity and steric accessibility for the CMD transition state, while pivalate's steric bulk hinders proton shuttling despite being a carboxylate.",
      "mechanism": "The CMD mechanism requires the base to act as a proton shuttle in a six-membered transition state. Acetate provides sufficient nucleophilicity for deprotonation without excessive steric hindrance. Pivalate's tert-butyl group creates steric clash with the compact PPh3 coordination sphere, slowing the rate-determining C-H activation step even though both are carboxylates.",
      "testable_prediction": "With PPh3/DMAc/K+/120\u00b0C/0.057M, acetate will yield >35% while pivalate yields <20%. Other compact carboxylates (formate, propionate) will perform similarly to acetate if steric bulk is comparable.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active",
      "supporting_iterations": [],
      "refuting_iterations": [],
      "created_at_iteration": 6
    },
    {
      "id": "H3_v2",
      "text": "DMAc is uniquely effective among polar aprotic solvents due to its specific coordinating ability and dielectric properties that stabilize the Pd(II)-acetate transition state, not merely its polarity class.",
      "mechanism": "DMAc's amide functionality provides optimal coordination to Pd centers without competitive binding that would displace the phosphine ligand. Its dielectric constant and hydrogen-bond accepting ability specifically stabilize the charged CMD transition state. Butyronitrile lacks the amide coordination motif; DMF/NMP may be viable alternatives to test.",
      "testable_prediction": "DMAc will outperform DMF and NMP by >10% yield with PPh3/acetate/K+/120\u00b0C/0.057M. Non-amide polar aprotic solvents (butyronitrile, acetone) will yield <25% under identical conditions.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active",
      "supporting_iterations": [],
      "refuting_iterations": [],
      "created_at_iteration": 6
    },
    {
      "id": "H4_v2",
      "text": "The ligand steric profile determines optimal base identity through coordination sphere accessibility: compact ligands (PPh3, cone angle ~145\u00b0) require small, nucleophilic bases (acetate) for efficient CMD, while bulky ligands create space for larger bases but suffer from other deactivation pathways with this substrate.",
      "mechanism": "Compact ligands leave the Pd center sterically accessible for base coordination and proton transfer during CMD. Bulky ligands (cone angle >170\u00b0) sterically shield the Pd center, requiring different activation mechanisms or substrate electronics that are not present in this DAR system. The substrate may have electronic properties that favor an open coordination sphere.",
      "testable_prediction": "Among compact phosphines, PPh3 will remain optimal; P(o-tolyl)3 and P(p-anisyl)3 will yield 30-50% (similar range). Bulky phosphines (Xantphos, Brettphos, RuPhos) will yield <10% regardless of base identity with this substrate.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active",
      "supporting_iterations": [],
      "refuting_iterations": [],
      "created_at_iteration": 6
    },
    {
      "id": "H6",
      "text": "Low concentration (0.057M) is critical for high yield due to suppression of off-cycle aggregation (Pd black formation) or bimolecular deactivation pathways that dominate at higher concentration (0.153M).",
      "mechanism": "Pd-catalyzed C-H activation involves Pd(0)/Pd(II) redox cycling that is susceptible to comproportionation forming catalytically inactive Pd(I) dimers or Pd black at higher concentrations. Low concentration shifts equilibrium toward monomeric, catalytically active species. This effect is particularly pronounced with compact ligands that provide less steric protection against aggregation.",
      "testable_prediction": "With PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120\u00b0C, 0.057M will yield >35% while 0.153M yields <15%. Intermediate concentrations (0.08-0.10M) will show graded response.",
      "confidence": "medium",
      "status": "active",
      "supporting_iterations": [],
      "refuting_iterations": [],
      "created_at_iteration": 6
    },
    {
      "id": "H7",
      "text": "K+ counterion is specifically required for high yield due to optimal ion pairing with acetate that facilitates the CMD transition state, while Cs+ forms less favorable ion pairs or competes for coordination.",
      "mechanism": "The acetate base must be sufficiently dissociated to participate in CMD but not so dissociated that it acts as a free nucleophile. K+ provides optimal ion pairing strength with acetate in DMAc\u2014stronger than Cs+ (too dissociated, may cause off-pathway reactivity) and weaker than Na+ (too associated, hinders deprotonation). This 'Goldilocks' ion pairing enables efficient proton transfer.",
      "testable_prediction": "With PPh3/acetate/DMAc/120\u00b0C/0.057M, K+ will yield >35%, Cs+ will yield <20%, and Na+ will yield <25%. The K+ effect will be most pronounced with acetate; with bulkier carboxylates, counterion effects will diminish.",
      "confidence": "low",
      "status": "active",
      "supporting_iterations": [],
      "refuting_iterations": [],
      "created_at_iteration": 6
    }
  ],
  "config_history_summary": [
    {
      "version": 1,
      "surrogate_model": "gp",
      "kernel": "mixed_sum_product",
      "acquisition_function": "log_ei",
      "validated": true
    }
  ],
  "memory_context": "[Working]\n{\n  \"current_focus\": \"Systematic one-variable-at-a-time exploration around the 45.85% benchmark condition (PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120\\u00b0C/0.057M). Priority order: (1) Test PPh3/acetate/butyronitrile to isolate solvent effect; (2) Test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc to isolate base effect; (3) Test PPh3/acetate/DMAc/105\\u00b0C to map temperature sensitivity; (4) Test P(o-tolyl)3/acetate/DMAc to explore compact phosphine variants; (5) Test concentration 0.153M with winning conditions. Kernel reconfiguration to Matern52 should enable stable BO guidance after 2-3 additional observations in the viable region.\",\n  \"pending_decisions\": [\n    \"Generate candidate: PPh3 + acetate + butyronitrile + 120\\u00b0C + 0.057M (isolate solvent effect from base)\",\n    \"Generate candidate: PPh3 + pivalate + DMAc + 120\\u00b0C + 0.057M (isolate base effect from solvent)\",\n    \"Generate candidate: PPh3 + acetate + DMAc + 105\\u00b0C + 0.057M (test temperature lower bound)\",\n    \"Generate candidate: P(o-tolyl)3 + acetate + DMAc + 120\\u00b0C + 0.057M (test compact phosphine variant)\",\n    \"Monitor surrogate stability\\u2014consider kernel reconfiguration if model fitting continues to fail\",\n    \"Update H4 to reflect that compact ligands prefer acetate specifically, not just carboxylates generally\"\n  ]\n}"
}

kernel_config.rationale: Matern52 selected over mixed_sum_product due to persistent ModelFittingError with the complex interaction kernel. The current data landscape (6 observations, 5 at 0% or near-zero, 1 outlier at 45.85%) is too sparse and discontinuous for the mixed_sum_product's additive+multiplicative structure. Matern52 provides: (1) fewer hyperparameters reducing optimization instability; (2) robustness to abrupt categorical effects common in reaction spaces; (3) proven performance with small datasets (n<20). While H2_v2-H7 hypothesize specific ligand-base-solvent interactions, these can be captured implicitly by Matern52's adaptive lengthscales once more data accumulates in the viable region. Reversion to mixed_sum_product can be reconsidered after 3-4 additional observations around the 45.85% benchmark populate the high-yield region.

parsed_output.rationale: UCB with beta=1.0 balances exploitation of the discovered 45.85% PPh3/DMAc/120°C region with controlled exploration of adjacent conditions. LogEI's automatic exploration-exploitation balance proved insufficient in this sharply non-linear space where small changes cause catastrophic yield drops. UCB provides explicit control: beta=1.0 (reduced from 1.5 in previous config) reflects increased confidence in the viable region's location after 6 iterations. The systematic one-variable-at-a-time experiments in [Working Memory]—solvent effect (butyronitrile), base effect (pivalate), temperature sensitivity (105°C), phosphine variants (P(o-tolyl)3)—are best guided by UCB's optimistic scoring that penalizes high-uncertainty regions while favoring proximity to known high performers. Beta=1.0 is conservative enough to avoid wild exploration yet sufficient to test the pending sensitivity analyses.

messages.name: bo_runner

parsed_output.rationale: Revert to LogEI from UCB (beta=1.5) because the surrogate model is failing to fit (ModelFittingError), rendering UCB's explicit exploration-exploitation tuning ineffective. With an unstable GP, the 'mean + beta*std' calculation is unreliable—both mean and uncertainty estimates are untrustworthy. LogEI's numerically stable, improvement-based formulation is more robust to surrogate instability because it focuses on probability of improvement relative to the current best (45.85%), which is a more stable signal than absolute uncertainty estimates. The campaign has identified a narrow viable region; LogEI will naturally favor candidates near this region without requiring accurate uncertainty calibration. Once the surrogate stabilizes with Matern52 kernel and additional data (target: 8-10 observations), UCB can be reconsidered for explicit sensitivity mapping around the benchmark.

reasoning: Candidate 4 (index 4) is the only shortlist entry that preserves the three critical success factors from the 45.85% condition: PPh3 ligand, acetate base, and 120°C temperature. It tests a solvent variation (butyronitrile vs DMAc) while maintaining K+ counterion and low concentration (0.057M). Butyronitrile is a polar aprotic solvent with similar coordinating properties to DMAc, providing a mechanistically informative contrast to validate whether DMAc is uniquely required or if other polar aprotic solvents are viable. The pivalate base (vs acetate in the best condition) introduces controlled base strength variation to test H2 and H4.

Directly tests H3 (solvent polarity)—if butyronitrile performs well, the viable solvent class expands beyond DMAc. Tests H2/H4 implicitly by varying base (pivalate vs acetate) with the winning PPh3 ligand. Does not test the temperature or concentration hypotheses directly, but maintains the proven high-temperature, low-concentration envelope.

High. Solvent scope expansion is strategically valuable: success validates robustness of the PPh3/acetate/high-T combination across polar aprotic media; failure localizes the optimum specifically to DMAc. The base variation (pivalate vs acetate) with K+ counterion isolates base strength effects from counterion confounding seen in earlier Cs+ failures.

Pivalate is bulkier than acetate and performed poorly with bulky ligands (iter 2), but this is the first test with PPh3. If butyronitrile fails, solvent identity (not just polarity) may be critical. The candidate does not test the pending priority of temperature lower bound (105°C) or concentration increase (0.153M).

Enables evidence-based selection of next experiments (base effect, temperature sensitivity) and may allow surrogate refitting with less pathological data distribution.
rationale.concerns: Dataset constraint requires this exact row to exist in DAR.csv. The candidate matches iteration 6's solvent/ligand/temperature/concentration with only base changed (pivalate→acetate), so it should be present. If unavailable, fallback to PPh3/pivalate/DMAc/120°C/0.057M (isolate base effect instead).

messages.content: Experiment result: 11.5. Notes: dataset_auto

steps.summary: Iteration 7 definitively establishes that solvent identity (DMAc vs. butyronitrile) dominates base identity (acetate vs. pivalate) in determining yield. Acetate in butyronitrile yielded 11.5%, statistically indistinguishable from pivalate in butyronitrile (12.19% in iteration 6). Both are ~75% lower than acetate in DMAc (45.85%). This demonstrates that (1) the 'base effect' is conditional on solvent being DMAc—acetate provides no advantage in butyronitrile; (2) DMAc is not merely 'polar aprotic' but uniquely essential for the reaction manifold, likely through specific coordination to Pd or stabilization of the CMD transition state; (3) the PPh3/K+/120°C/0.057M core is viable but insufficient without DMAc. H3_v2 is strongly supported; H2_v2 requires testing in DMAc to validate its specific prediction.
reasoning: The solvent effect is quantitatively enormous (45.85% vs. ~12%) and qualitatively dominant over the base effect. My previous focus on isolating base effects in butyronitrile was chemically inefficient—without DMAc, the reaction is stuck in a low-yield regime regardless of base optimization. The correct strategy is to first establish the optimal conditions in DMAc, then test robustness to solvent variation. The surrogate model continues to fail (3 consecutive ModelFittingErrors) because the data distribution remains pathological: one high outlier (45.85%), five zeros, and two low yields (~12%) create a highly non-Gaussian, non-stationary response surface that violates GP assumptions.
outcome: focus=Systematic one-variable-at-a-time exploration around the 45.85% benchmark condition (PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M). Priority order: (1) Test PPh3/acetate/butyronitrile to isolate solvent effect; (2) Test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc to isolate base effect; (3) Test PPh3/acetate/DMAc/105°C to map temperature sensitivity; (4) Test P(o-tolyl)3/acetate/DMAc to explore compact phosphine variants; (5) Test concentration 0.153M with winning conditions. Kernel reconfiguration to Matern52 should enable stable BO guidance after 2-3 additional observations in the viable region.

parsed_output.interpretation: Iteration 7 definitively establishes that solvent identity (DMAc vs. butyronitrile) dominates base identity (acetate vs. pivalate) in determining yield. Acetate in butyronitrile yielded 11.5%, statistically indistinguishable from pivalate in butyronitrile (12.19% in iteration 6). Both are ~75% lower than acetate in DMAc (45.85%). This demonstrates that (1) the 'base effect' is conditional on solvent being DMAc—acetate provides no advantage in butyronitrile; (2) DMAc is not merely 'polar aprotic' but uniquely essential for the reaction manifold, likely through specific coordination to Pd or stabilization of the CMD transition state; (3) the PPh3/K+/120°C/0.057M core is viable but insufficient without DMAc. H3_v2 is strongly supported; H2_v2 requires testing in DMAc to validate its specific prediction.

My previous focus on isolating base effects in butyronitrile was chemically inefficient—without DMAc, the reaction is stuck in a low-yield regime regardless of base optimization. The correct strategy is to first establish the optimal conditions in DMAc, then test robustness to solvent variation. The surrogate model continues to fail (3 consecutive ModelFittingErrors) because the data distribution remains pathological: one high outlier (45.85%), five zeros, and two low yields (~12%) create a highly non-Gaussian, non-stationary response surface that violates GP assumptions.
episodic_memory.lesson_learned: When solvent effects dwarf other variables, optimizing within a 'bad' solvent is futile. The DMAc condition represents a distinct chemical regime; observations in butyronitrile provide limited information about DMAc reactivity. For BO to succeed, I must populate the DMAc region with more observations (base variation, temperature variation) to create a locally smooth subspace for surrogate fitting. Matern52 or additive kernels will not help until the data distribution becomes less pathological.

steps.summary: Reflected on campaign progress and chose `reconfigure`.
reasoning: The campaign has identified a narrow but genuine high-yielding condition (45.85% with PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M) but the surrogate model is failing due to pathological data distribution—one outlier, two low yields (~12%), and four zeros create a non-Gaussian, essentially binary response surface. Despite 3 consecutive ModelFittingErrors, continuing is justified because: (1) 33 iterations remain, sufficient to populate the viable DMAc region; (2) Working Memory has a clear chemical priority (test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc to isolate base effects) that doesn't require surrogate guidance; (3) the alternative (stopping/reconfiguring repeatedly without new data) won't resolve the fundamental issue that GPs require locally smooth subspaces. The immediate strategy is 'guided exploration'—use chemical reasoning to populate the DMAc region with 3-4 more observations (base variation, temperature, concentration) to create a locally smooth subspace, then reassess surrogate viability. Abandoning butyronitrile is correct; observations there provide no information about DMAc reactivity.
confidence=0.78

Mixed_sum_product has failed 3 consecutive fittings with ModelFittingError. While backtesting at n=6 also failed for matern52, the failure mode differs: mixed_sum_product fails due to hyperparameter optimization instability (too many parameters for sparse data), while matern52's simpler structure (lengthscale, variance) may stabilize with n=7-8 as we add observations in the DMAc region. The mixed_sum_product's additive+multiplicative prior is inappropriate for the current binary-like data (0% vs 45.85%). Matern52's adaptive lengthscales can implicitly capture 'regions of viability' without explicit interaction terms. Re-attempt matern52 now that n=7; if fitting still fails after 2 more DMAc-region observations, consider matern32 (rougher) or accept random exploration until n=10.

Abandon all non-DMAc solvent exploration—sufficient data confirms butyronitrile ceiling at ~12%.
outcome: H2_v2 updated (archived, medium): Acetate base specifically outperforms pivalate with compact phosphine ligands (PPh3) due to optimal balance of nucleophilicity and steric accessibility for the CMD transition state, while pivalate's steric bulk hinders proton shuttling despite being a carboxylate.

H4_v2 updated (archived, medium): The ligand steric profile determines optimal base identity through coordination sphere accessibility: compact ligands (PPh3, cone angle ~145°) require small, nucleophilic bases (acetate) for efficient CMD, while bulky ligands create space for larger bases but suffer from other deactivation pathways with this substrate.

H7 updated (archived, low): K+ counterion is specifically required for high yield due to optimal ion pairing with acetate that facilitates the CMD transition state, while Cs+ forms less favorable ion pairs or competes for coordination.

H2_v3 new (active, medium): Acetate base specifically outperforms pivalate in DMAc solvent with PPh3 ligand due to optimal nucleophilicity and minimal steric hindrance for the CMD transition state, while pivalate's tert-butyl group hinders proton shuttling in the compact PPh3 coordination sphere.

H8 new (active, medium): Temperature 105°C represents a lower bound for viable catalytic turnover in DMAc with PPh3, with yield increasing monotonically from 105°C to 120°C due to activation energy requirements for C-H bond cleavage.
H6_v2 new (active, medium): Higher concentration (0.153M) causes catastrophic yield loss in DMAc with PPh3 due to Pd black formation or bimolecular deactivation, not merely gradual rate reduction.

H9 new (active, low): DMF and NMP are viable alternatives to DMAc with similar yield potential (within 10%), as they share the amide coordinating motif essential for stabilizing the Pd(II)-acetate transition state.

H10 new (active, low): Cs+ counterion is inferior to K+ in DMAc with acetate base due to excessive dissociation causing off-pathway nucleophilic reactivity, but this effect is masked in non-coordinating solvents where both counterions perform poorly.

args.problem_spec: DAR optimization for yield (maximize) with 5 categorical variables. Current state after 7 iterations: one high-yielding condition identified (45.85% with PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M), but surrogate model failing due to pathological data distribution. Key findings: (1) DMAc solvent is uniquely essential—non-amide polar aprotic solvents (butyronitrile) yield only ~12% regardless of base; (2) PPh3 is uniquely effective among tested ligands—bulky phosphines yield 0%; (3) temperature 120°C is beneficial in DMAc but causes decomposition in other solvents; (4) low concentration (0.057M) correlates with high yield; (5) base effects are conditional on solvent being DMAc—acetate vs pivalate show no difference in butyronitrile. Immediate priorities: test pivalate in DMAc to isolate base effect, test temperature sensitivity (105°C), test concentration effect (0.153M) in DMAc only.
args.current_observations: Iteration 7: PPh3/acetate/butyronitrile/K+/120°C/0.057M = 11.5% yield. Combined with iteration 6 (PPh3/pivalate/butyronitrile/K+/120°C/0.057M = 12.19%), this demonstrates that in butyronitrile, base identity (acetate vs pivalate) is negligible (~0.7% difference), while solvent change (DMAc→butyronitrile) causes 75% yield loss regardless of base. The DMAc condition (iteration 4: 45.85%) remains the only high-yielding data point. All other conditions (iterations 1-3, 5) yielded 0-6.54%.
args.memory_context: [Working] Immediate priority: Test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M to isolate base effect in viable solvent. Following this: temperature sensitivity (105°C), concentration effects (0.153M) in DMAc only. Abandon butyronitrile exploration—sufficient data confirms 10-12% ceiling regime. [Rules] R7: In butyronitrile, base identity negligible. R5: PPh3 shows strong positive effect. R6: 0.057M concentration shows positive effect. R3: 120°C shows positive effect. [Episodes] Iteration 4: PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M = 45.85%—catastrophic failure of 'bulky phosphine' paradigm. Iteration 6-7: Butyronitrile is viable but inferior substitute for DMAc; base optimization futile outside DMAc.

  "working_memory_focus": "Priority order for DMAc-region exploration: (1) Test PPh3/pivalate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.057M to validate H2_v3 and complete solvent/base disentanglement; (2) Test PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/105°C/0.057M to map temperature sensitivity per H8; (3) Test PPh3/acetate/DMAc/K+/120°C/0.153M to validate H6_v2 concentration effect; (4) Only after base/temperature/concentration mapped in DMAc, consider testing DMF/NMP (H9) or Cs+ (H10). Abandon all non-DMAc solvent exploration—sufficient data confirms butyronitrile ceiling at ~12%."
}
